children.name: Taxi Driver
children.reason: Scorsese's masterpiece captures the psychological rot beneath the city's decay—Travis Bickle as the embodiment of urban alienation stalking through neon-lit squalor.

children.name: The Friends of Eddie Coyle
children.reason: Peter Yates' Boston crime film shares Taxi Driver's vision of urban decay eating away at working-class masculinity—both films understand that the city doesn't just contain violence, it creates it.

children.name: Suicide
children.reason: Alan Vega and Martin Rev's two-man electronic assault unit channeled the city's industrial violence into sound—their drum machines and synthesizers were the heartbeat of a dying metropolis, not music but urban life support.

children.name: Frankie Teardrop
children.reason: Ten minutes of electronic terror that follows a factory worker's descent into madness and murder—the sound of what happens when the American Dream meets New York reality and explodes.

children.name: The Power Broker
children.reason: Robert Caro's biography of Robert Moses reveals how one man's vision created the infrastructure that would decay into 70s New York—the highways that carved up neighborhoods, the housing projects that became vertical ghettos.

children.name: Co-op City
children.reason: The Bronx housing complex that Caro wrote about—35 brutalist towers housing 60,000 people, Moses' utopian vision turned dystopian reality, where middle-class families found themselves trapped in what critics called 'vertical suburbs of despair.'

children.name: New York Dolls at the Mercer Arts Center Collapse
children.reason: The night the decrepit Broadway hotel housing the city's premier glam rock venue literally fell down, crushing four people—a perfect metaphor for how the city's cultural renaissance was built on rotting foundations.

children.name: Oscar Wilde Room
children.reason: The tiny basement venue within the Mercer Arts Center where the New York Dolls, Television, and dozens of other bands played to crowds of 50—the primordial scene before CBGB, where glam and punk were still indistinguishable.

searchHint.title: Oscar Wilde Room Mercer Arts Center

children.name: Alphabet City
children.reason: The Lower East Side neighborhood east of Avenue A where buildings sold for $1000 and junkies nodded off in doorways—the city's designated sacrifice zone, where artists and criminals shared the same abandoned tenements.

children.name: Loisaida
children.reason: The Puerto Rican name for the Lower East Side that became a rallying cry—poet Bittman Roth's Spanish-English portmanteau capturing how Latino families held onto community even as their neighborhood crumbled around them.

children.name: Colson Whitehead
children.reason: The novelist who grew up in 70s Manhattan and later wrote about how the city's decay shaped his childhood imagination—his memoir 'The Colossus of New York' maps the psychological geography of a kid navigating urban collapse.

children.name: The Colossus of New York
children.reason: Whitehead's love letter to the city is also an elegy for the dangerous, broke New York of his youth—each essay captures a different piece of urban architecture and the memories embedded in concrete and steel.
